original_problem:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
problem_solved:
After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set theorem: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), the relative Dressian \(\Dr(M)\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, admits an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\) extending the flat-lattice involution and satisfying
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).
strategy_summary: The proof begins by reducing to the case of a simple matroid via its simplification, then exploits the fact that a simple matroid whose flat lattice admits an order-reversing involution is modular.  It shows any valuated quotient of the matroid is constant on each flat and uses modularity to define a “dagger” dual of a valuated quotient by reversing ranks along the lattice involution.  One checks that this dagger construction again satisfies the valuated‐quotient (circuit) criterion and the tropical Plücker relations, and is involutive.  Finally, an annihilator argument proves that dagger depends only on the tropical linear space, not on its Plücker vector, so it descends to an order-reversing involution on the relative Dressian extending the given flat–lattice involution and swapping contracted flats as required.
solution:
Below is a polished end-to-end proof. I have kept the argument close to the candidate proof, but reorganized it into lemmas and filled in the main gaps: the simplification reduction, the modularity reduction, the proof that the “dagger” construction is a valuated quotient, the well-definedness on tropical linear spaces rather than on Plücker vectors, and the verification on the embedded flat lattice.

I use standard facts from valuated matroid theory: valuated matroids may be characterized by tropical Plücker relations / valuated basis exchange; tropical linear spaces are equivalently described by valuated circuits or cocircuits; and tropical linear spaces are tropically convex. These facts go back to Dress–Wenzel and Murota–Tamura, and are used in Speyer’s and Maclagan–Sturmfels’ treatments of tropical linear spaces. For bibliographic data, see Dress–Wenzel as cited in Speyer’s reference list, Speyer’s paper on tropical linear spaces, Murota–Tamura’s circuit-valuation paper, and Hampe’s paper on tropical linear spaces and tropical convexity. ([epubs.siam.org](https://epubs.siam.org/doi/pdf/10.1137/080716219?utm_source=openai))

---

# Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\longrightarrow \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(\Dr(M)\) denotes the poset, ordered by inclusion, of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\).

---

# Proof

We use the min-plus convention. Put
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
All tropical projective spaces are obtained from affine cones in \(\mathbb T^E\) by quotienting by tropical scalar translation. When checking equations, we allow the all-\(\infty\) vector in the affine cone; it represents no projective point and causes no difficulty.

For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), we write
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\longrightarrow \mathbb T,
\]
not identically \(\infty\), and extend \(p\) to all subsets by setting \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\neq k\). Its tropical linear space is
\[
\Trop(p)
=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}:
\text{ for every }J\in \binom{E}{k+1},
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
An ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

A valuated matroid \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of \(M\) if
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]
Thus \(\Dr(M)\) is the set of projective tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\), where \(p\) ranges over valuated quotients of \(M\), ordered by inclusion.

We shall write \(\operatorname{rk}_M\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_M\) for the rank and closure operators of \(M\). For flats \(G,H\), we use lattice notation
\[
G\wedge H=G\cap H,
\qquad
G\vee H=\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup H).
\]

---

## 1. A quotient criterion

We first record the basic criterion for a valuated matroid to be a quotient of an ordinary matroid.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e}.
\]
Here \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

### Lemma 1.1 — Circuit criterion for quotients

For \(k>0\), the following are equivalent:

1. \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M)\).
2. For every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), the minimum
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

For \(k=0\), the tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\) is empty projectively, so \(p\) is automatically a quotient of \(M\).

### Proof

The ordinary tropical linear space \(\Trop(M)\) is cut out by the circuit equations
\[
\min_{c\in C} x_c
\quad
\text{has no unique finite minimizer}
\]
for all circuits \(C\) of \(M\).

On the other hand, \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits \(C_A\). Tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex. Therefore \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M)\) if and only if every valuated cocircuit \(C_A\) satisfies every circuit equation of \(M\). For a circuit \(C\), this condition is exactly
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
having no unique finite minimizer. ∎

We shall use three immediate consequences.

### Lemma 1.2 — Support consequences

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the \(k\)-subsets \(I\subseteq E\) with \(p_I<\infty\). Then:

1. If \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\).
2. \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), in the sense that
   \[
   \operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
   \qquad
   \text{for all }A\subseteq E.
   \]
3. If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
   \[
   \operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
   \le
   \operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
   \]

### Proof

For (1), suppose \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), and choose \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\). By Lemma 1.1, the one-term minimum \(p_{A\cup c}=p_I\) cannot have a unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p_I=\infty\).

For (2), assume \(k>0\); the rank-zero case is trivial. We show that every circuit of \(M\) is a union of circuits of \(N\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. Suppose, for contradiction, that some \(e\in C\) is a coloop of \(N|C\). Then \(e\notin \operatorname{cl}_N(C-e)\), so there exists an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\). Choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\), so \(|A|=k-1\). Then \(A\cup e\) is an \(N\)-basis, while \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent for every \(c\in C-e\). Thus
\[
p_{A\cup e}<\infty,
\qquad
p_{A\cup c}=\infty
\quad(c\in C-e),
\]
contradicting Lemma 1.1. Therefore no element of \(C\) is a coloop of \(N|C\), and hence every element of \(C\) lies in an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\). Thus \(C\) is a union of \(N\)-circuits.

Now let \(e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Then there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup e\) containing \(e\). Since \(C\) is a union of \(N\)-circuits, one of those \(N\)-circuits contains \(e\); it is contained in \(A\cup e\). Hence \(e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq \operatorname{cl}_N(A).
\]
It follows that
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

For (3), choose a subset \(S\subseteq H\) such that \(G\cup S\) spans \(H\) in \(M\) and
\[
|S|=\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Then, using (2),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(G\cup S)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_N(G)+|S|,
\]
which gives the desired inequality. ∎

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

We next reduce to the case where \(M\) is simple.

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\), and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\). The ground set \(\bar E\) of \(S\) is the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\longrightarrow \bar E
\]
be the quotient map.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of nonloop parallel classes. Hence
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
The given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) therefore induces one on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This descends to tropical projective space.

### Lemma 2.1 — Simplification induces an isomorphism of relative Dressians

The map \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

### Proof

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). By Lemma 1.2, every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-set has \(p\)-value \(\infty\). In particular:

- if \(e\) is a loop of \(M\), then every \(k\)-set containing \(e\) has value \(\infty\);
- if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then every \(k\)-set containing both \(e\) and \(f\) has value \(\infty\).

Also, if \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. Lemma 1.1 gives, for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) disjoint from \(\{e,f\}\),
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Therefore \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I.
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and the quotient criterion for \(S\) follows from the quotient criterion for \(M\), because circuits of \(S\) are precisely the images of nonloop circuits of \(M\) after deleting parallel repetitions. Hence \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi\text{ is injective on }I,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) either reduce to those for \(q\), or are trivial because all terms are \(\infty\) or occur in equal parallel pairs. The quotient criterion for \(M\) follows from the quotient criterion for \(S\), together with the loop circuits and two-element parallel circuits of \(M\). Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Finally,
\[
\Trop(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\Trop(q)).
\]
Indeed, the loop equations impose \(x_e=\infty\) for \(e\in L_0\), and the parallel-pair equations impose equality of the coordinates in each nonloop parallel class. After these identifications, the remaining equations are exactly the equations for \(q\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) identifies quotient tropical linear spaces of \(S\) with quotient tropical linear spaces of \(M\), and it preserves inclusions. ∎

Moreover, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Thus it is enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple. We do this next.

---

## 3. The simple case: modularity

Assume from now on that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). The rank-zero case is trivial, so assume \(r>0\). Since \(M\) is simple, \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice. In particular it is ranked and semimodular.

Because \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution, its dual lattice is isomorphic to \(\mathcal L(M)\). Hence the dual lattice is also semimodular. Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is both upper and lower semimodular. Equivalently, for all flats \(G,H\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Thus \(M\) is modular.

Also, the involution reverses ranks:
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
\qquad
(F\in\mathcal L(M)).
\]
Indeed, an order-reversing bijection sends maximal chains from \(\varnothing\) to \(F\) to maximal chains from \(F^\perp\) to \(E\).

---

## 4. Flat-constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

By Lemma 1.2, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I,I'\in\binom{E}{k}\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(F\). We claim that
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]
It is enough to prove this when
\[
I=A\cup a,
\qquad
I'=A\cup b,
\]
because the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected.

Since
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab)=F,
\]
there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying Lemma 1.1 to \(C\) and \(A\), the minimum of
\[
p_{A\cup a},\quad p_{A\cup b}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the possibility that both are \(\infty\). Therefore \(p_I=p_{I'}\).

Thus, for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
where \(I\) is any \(M\)-basis of \(F\).

---

## 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and set
\[
n=r-k.
\]
We define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),
& J\in\binom{E}{n}\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
& J\in\binom{E}{n}\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
We shall prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M\), up to adding a scalar. If \(k=r\), flat-constancy forces \(p\) to be the constant valuated matroid of \(M\), again up to scalar, and \(p^\dagger\) is the unique rank-zero valuated matroid. Thus assume
\[
0<k<r.
\]

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), a rank \(k\) quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5.1. The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Define a function on flats \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\) by
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and extend it to all subsets by
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function of rank \(n\).

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
=
0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)
=
0,
\]
and
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)
=
r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)
=
n.
\]

Let \(G\subseteq H\) be flats of \(M\). Then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). By Lemma 1.2,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp).
\]
Since ranks are reversed by \(\perp\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Therefore
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)-\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0.
\]
So \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone on flats, hence on all subsets.

Next let \(G,H\) be flats. Modularity of \(M\) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp).
\]
Now
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), Lemma 1.2 gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\wedge H)^\perp).
\]
Thus
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
So \(\rho^\dagger\) is submodular on flats.

For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\subseteq E\), put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
Using monotonicity and the flat submodularity just proved, we get
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)+\rho^\dagger(B)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(A\cap B)+\rho^\dagger(A\cup B).
\]
So \(\rho^\dagger\) is submodular.

Finally,
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)
\le
|A|.
\]
Also \(\rho^\dagger(A)\ge 0\) by monotonicity. Submodularity gives
\[
\rho^\dagger(A\cup e)-\rho^\dagger(A)
\le
\rho^\dagger(\{e\})-\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
\le 1.
\]
Hence \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

Let \(N^\dagger\) be the resulting rank \(n\) matroid.

For \(J\in\binom{E}{n}\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) if and only if
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n.
\]
Since
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n,
\]
this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. For such \(J\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)
=
n-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\) if and only if
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Therefore the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is precisely the set of bases of \(N^\dagger\).

---

### 5.2. Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Because the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is a matroid, it is enough to verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations. Thus let
\[
S\in\binom{E}{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all six \(n\)-sets appearing above are \(M\)-dependent, so all three terms are \(\infty\).

If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then in each displayed summand at least one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent. Again all three terms are \(\infty\).

So assume \(S\) is independent and
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(X)=n-2.
\]
Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), so \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent, and therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty.
\]
If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then \(Sef\) is \(M\)-independent, and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(Sef)^\perp
=
H_e\wedge H_f.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

Therefore the desired Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

---

### Lemma 5.3 — Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Proof

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Hence the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not unique if finite.

Thus assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since we are in a modular rank \(k+2\) interval and the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes, we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}_M(Z)=k\)

Each pairwise intersection \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) and contains \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). Therefore all three sums are equal.

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}_M(Z)=k-2\)

Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
In the interval \([Z,Y]\), each \(T_i\) has relative rank \(1\), i.e.
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(T_i)=k-1.
\]
Indeed, the meet of three hyperplanes in a modular rank-four interval has relative rank at least \(1\). If it had relative rank at least \(2\), then meeting with the fourth hyperplane would still have positive relative rank, contradicting the definition of \(Z\).

If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each \(i\), choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then, for example,
\[
[12]=p_{A t_3 t_4},
\qquad
[34]=p_{A t_1 t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other pairings. Thus the desired relation is exactly the three-term tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}_M(Z)=k-1\)

Then \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three modular geometry. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define a function on these points by
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We first show that
\[
w\in \Trop([Z,Y]).
\]
Let \(D\) be a circuit in the simple rank-three geometry \([Z,Y]\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the circuit-lifting property for contraction by \(Z\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying Lemma 1.1 to \(C\) and \(A\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute only \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in \Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use the following elementary rank-three fact.

---

### Claim — Rank-three matching fact

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\Trop(G)\) be a weight function on its points. If \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are distinct lines of \(G\), then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Proof of the claim

For a vector \(w\) on the points of a simple matroid, the condition \(w\in\Trop(G)\) is equivalent to the condition that every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. Indeed, a unique minimum on a circuit is exactly the obstruction to the corresponding superlevel set being closed.

In rank three, the proper nonempty flats are points and lines. Since the superlevel sets are nested, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a chain
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line; either may be absent. After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\). Hence the cost of pairing \(i,j\) is a nonnegative number depending only on the common color \(y_i=y_j\), and is \(0\) if the colors differ. For four colored objects, among the three perfect matchings the minimum total equal-color cost is never unique. This is immediate from the possible color multiplicities:
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]

Now suppose one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(i=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\lambda+\mu,&y=a,\\
\mu,&y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three values \(c(y_i)\) are minimal, so the minimum is not unique. If exactly two of the \(y_i\) are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. This proves the claim. ∎

Returning to Case 3, apply the claim to the rank-three geometry \([Z,Y]\), with lines
\[
L_i=H_i/Z.
\]
The points \(L_i\wedge L_j\) correspond exactly to the flats \(H_i\wedge H_j\), and the claim gives the desired relation. ∎

This proves Lemma 5.3, and hence the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

---

### 5.4. The quotient condition for \(p^\dagger\)

It remains to prove
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]
By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to check the circuit criterion.

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\in\binom{E}{n-1}.
\]
We must prove that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is \(M\)-dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(X)=n-1.
\]
Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)=k+1.
\]

For every point \(P\) of the contraction \(M/X\), equivalently every rank \(n\) flat \(P\) of \(M\) with \(X<P\), define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Here \(P^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat contained in \(Y\).

We claim that
\[
u\in \Trop(M/X)
\]
on the simple point geometry of \(M/X\).

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat.

In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, suppose a set \(S\) of points is line-closed. We show by induction on \(\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl} S)\) that \(S\) contains every point of its closure. Choose an independent spanning set
\[
a_1,\dots,a_m\subseteq S
\]
for \(\operatorname{cl} S\), and let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, all points of \(H\) lie in \(S\). If \(x\in\operatorname{cl} S\setminus H\), then modularity implies that the line \(\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Since \(h,a_m\in S\) and \(S\) is line-closed, we get \(x\in S\).

So let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be any point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\). Since
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2,
\]
we have
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]

If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\). Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\), all containing
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
which has rank \(k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
e_i\in H_i\setminus Z
\]
such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([Z,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying Lemma 1.1 to \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\).

Now return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), then for every \(c\in C\setminus A\), the set \(A\cup c\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Otherwise set
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
For \(c\in C\setminus A\) with \(c\in X\), the term is \(\infty\).

We claim every \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D-c)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(X\cup C-c)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(X\cup C)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Hence \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\), and therefore lies in a circuit contained in \(D\).

If the minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then restricting to a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would contradict \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(M).
\]

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Dagger is an involution

We now prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 1.2, and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\) by definition.

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F\) is a rank \(k\) flat. We have
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of the rank \(n\) flat \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

---

## 7. Effect on flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is
\[
r-\operatorname{rk}_M(F).
\]

Let \(G\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), which is equivalent to
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Because
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(F)=r
\]
and \(M\) is modular, the condition \(G\vee F=E\) is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

The polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) exchanges complements: \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 8. The annihilator description

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) depends only on the tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\), not on the particular valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis \(B^*\) by
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp\) is a rank-one flat, hence a single element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\), we have
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp.
\]
The flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(T)\), \(T\subseteq B\), form a Boolean sublattice, so
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
Thus the displayed identity follows.

In particular \(B^*\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\). Hence
\[
B\longmapsto B^*
\]
is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
The support \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) is a quotient of \(M\), so every \(M\)-basis contains an \(N\)-basis. Hence \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\):
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

We shall use the following standard annihilator fact.

### Lemma 8.1 — Ordinary tropical annihilator

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(Q\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^Q\), define the tropical hyperplane
\[
H_u=
\{y:\min_{i\in Q}(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

### Proof

The circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\). Thus \(u\in\Trop(\mu^*)\) exactly means that \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\).

But \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex. Therefore \(u\) is orthogonal to all cocircuits of \(\mu\) if and only if the whole tropical linear space \(\Trop(\mu)\) lies in \(H_u\). ∎

Now let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Regard \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), and define
\[
x^\perp:=x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(x^\perp\) is a rank \(r-1\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp).
\tag{\(*\)}
\]

### Proof of \((*)\)

Assume first that
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
By Lemma 8.1,
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\Trop(\mu_B)\), unless all coordinates of \(y|_B\) are \(\infty\), in which case the following hyperplane condition is automatic. Hence
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

For the rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid \(x^\perp\), one has
\[
(x^\perp)_{B-b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore the defining tropical equation for \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\) holds on every \(M\)-basis \(B\).

It remains to check the defining equations for dependent \(r\)-subsets \(K\subseteq E\). If
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2,
\]
then every \((r-1)\)-subset \(K-e\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1,
\]
then \(K\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(x^\perp\) occur exactly for \(e\in D\), and for those \(e\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(K-e)
\]
is the same rank \(r-1\) flat. Hence the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(D\) satisfied by \(y\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), this holds.

Thus \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\). Since \(y\in\Trop(p)\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show that
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]

By Lemma 8.1, it is enough to show that every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) lies in \(H_{u_B}\). Let
\[
S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\},
\qquad
|S|=k-1,
\]
and put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
i\longmapsto p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
e\longmapsto p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If this vector is identically \(\infty\), there is nothing to check. Otherwise it is a point of \(\Trop(p)\), hence a point of \(\Trop(x^\perp)\). Testing the defining equation of \(\Trop(x^\perp)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. This is exactly the condition that the cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) lies in \(H_{u_B}\). Therefore
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, it follows that \(x\) satisfies the defining equations of \(p^\dagger\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

Now let
\[
J\in\binom{E}{n+1}.
\]
We verify the defining equation for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on \(J\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis. The equation follows from the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n-1,
\]
then every \(n\)-subset \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally, suppose
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=n.
\]
Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\), the flats
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
are all equal. Thus the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(D\) satisfied by \(x\in\Trop(M)\).

Therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves \((*)\). ∎

Consequently,
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M):\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)\}.
\tag{\(**\)}
\]
The right-hand side depends only on \(\Trop(p)\). Hence the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to tropical linear spaces.

---

## 9. Construction of \(\Phi\) in the simple case

Let
\[
X\in\Dr(M).
\]
Choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
By \((**)\), this is independent of the choice of \(p\).

Since dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Moreover, if
\[
X\subseteq Y,
\]
then for any \(x\in\Phi(Y)\), equation \((**)\) gives
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]
Hence
\[
X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp),
\]
so \(x\in\Phi(X)\). Thus
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by the computation in Section 7, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

## 10. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\). By Lemma 2.1, the map
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism. The given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\longrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced involution on flats.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\longrightarrow\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\). Also,
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Phi_M\!\left(
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Phi_S\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Trop(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) has the required property.

This completes the proof. ∎

---
